title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about the rise and impact of the sharing economy. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Ownership Reconsidered
content.subtitle: A business magazine article examining the economic and social implications of the sharing economy.
content.text: In 2008, two designers in San Francisco, unable to afford their rent, placed three air mattresses on their living room floor and rented them to conference delegates who couldn't find hotel rooms. Within five years, the company they built from that idea was valued at more than the world's largest hotel chains. [31] The story of Airbnb has been told so often that it has acquired the smooth, slightly improbable quality of a modern myth — which should perhaps be the first reason for examining it more carefully. [/31] || The 'sharing economy' — a term that covers everything from short-term accommodation rental to car-sharing, tool libraries, and freelance labour platforms — arrived with ambitious promises. Advocates argued that it would make better use of underutilised assets, reduce consumption, lower costs for consumers, and provide flexible income opportunities for ordinary people. In some cases, these promises were delivered. In others, closer examination reveals a considerably more complicated picture. [32] The label 'sharing' has proved particularly contentious, with critics arguing that it applies a language of community and mutual benefit to what is, in most cases, simply a new form of commercial transaction. [/32] || Economist Dr Lena Fischer, whose recent book examines the labour dimensions of digital platform businesses, is among those who are sceptical of the 'sharing' framing. Her research focuses on the workers who provide services through platforms — the drivers, cleaners and handypeople whose labour underlies the consumer-facing convenience. [33] 'The flexibility that platforms advertise is real,' she acknowledges, 'but flexibility and security are not the same thing, and the marketing language of these companies tends to blur that distinction.' [/33] || The regulatory challenges posed by the sharing economy have tested governments around the world. Traditional industries — taxi companies, hotels, rental agencies — have faced disruption from competitors who operate outside established licensing and employment frameworks. Cities from New York to Barcelona have moved to limit or regulate short-term rental platforms, citing their effect on housing availability and rental prices in residential neighbourhoods. [34] The platforms, for their part, have generally argued that regulation stifles innovation and consumer choice — a position that regulators and community groups have found increasingly difficult to accept. [/34] || Not all sharing-economy developments have attracted the same level of controversy. Models in which participants genuinely share rather than sell — neighbourhood tool libraries, food-sharing networks, community car pools — have been widely welcomed as examples of the original collaborative ideal. [35] These models, however, typically remain small-scale precisely because they do not generate the commercial returns that attract investment and enable growth. [/35] The very mechanism that would allow them to scale undermines the values that make them distinctive. || The sharing economy is now old enough to be assessed not by its promises but by its consequences. [36] What the evidence suggests is that the sector has created genuine value in some areas while reproducing or intensifying existing inequalities in others — a finding that resists both the cheerful optimism of its early advocates and the wholesale condemnation of its critics. [/36]

questions.question: The writer describes the Airbnb origin story as resembling 'a modern myth' in order to suggest that
options: A) the story of the company's founding has been significantly exaggerated over time.
B) its familiar, polished quality should prompt readers to look at it more critically.
C) stories about company founders are always more entertaining than they are accurate.
D) Airbnb's success was largely the result of good fortune rather than good design.
questions.explanation: The writer uses 'should perhaps be the first reason for examining it more carefully', signalling that the myth-like smoothness of the story warrants scrutiny rather than acceptance.

questions.question: The critics mentioned in the second paragraph object to the word 'sharing' because
options: A) it suggests that participants receive goods and services without paying for them.
B) it makes profit-driven commercial activity sound like community cooperation.
C) it implies that sharing economy platforms do not generate tax revenue.
D) it misleads investors about the financial risks involved in digital platforms.
questions.explanation: Critics argue the term 'sharing' applies 'a language of community and mutual benefit' to what is actually 'a new form of commercial transaction' — the problem is the misleading community framing.

questions.question: Dr Fischer's distinction between flexibility and security is intended to show that
options: A) platform workers earn less than employees in traditional industries.
B) the advantages marketed to platform workers conceal significant drawbacks.
C) flexibility is not actually something that most workers want from their jobs.
D) security of employment should be legally required for all workers.
questions.explanation: Dr Fischer acknowledges flexibility is real but argues it is not the same as security — showing that the benefit being advertised ('flexibility') hides what is absent ('security'), implying the marketing is misleading.

questions.question: Cities that have moved to regulate short-term rental platforms are responding primarily to
options: A) pressure from traditional hotel and accommodation businesses.
B) concerns about the effect on housing availability for local residents.
C) evidence that platform operators are avoiding paying business taxes.
D) dissatisfaction with the quality of accommodation offered through platforms.
questions.explanation: The text states cities are acting 'citing their effect on housing availability and rental prices in residential neighbourhoods' — the housing impact is the stated reason for regulation.

questions.question: The writer mentions genuine sharing models such as tool libraries to illustrate
options: A) the kind of successful community organisation that the sharing economy originally promised.
B) the contradiction between the values of community sharing and the need for commercial growth.
C) the fact that non-commercial models are more popular with consumers than profit-driven ones.
D) evidence that the sharing economy has had a positive impact on local communities.
questions.explanation: The writer notes these models stay small 'because they do not generate commercial returns' and that the mechanism enabling growth 'undermines the values that make them distinctive' — a direct contradiction between values and scale.

questions.question: The writer's overall conclusion about the sharing economy is that it
options: A) has largely failed to deliver on its original promises to consumers.
B) is too recent a phenomenon to draw any firm conclusions about.
C) has produced both genuine benefits and significant negative effects.
D) has been more harmful to society than its early supporters acknowledged.
questions.explanation: The final paragraph says the sector 'has created genuine value in some areas while reproducing or intensifying existing inequalities in others' — a balanced assessment acknowledging both positive and negative outcomes.
